title: 2 Superior Growth Stocks to Buy in 2026
author: John Ballard
description: Nvidia and Meta Platforms are recommended as top growth stocks for 2026. Nvidia, the leading AI hardware supplier, shows strong revenue growth of 85% YoY with expanding product lines and a forward P/E of 23 that appears undervalued relative to its 45% expected earnings growth. Meta's 33% YoY revenue growth, driven by AI-powered advertising improvements and a massive 3.5 billion daily user base, positions it for continued monetization despite heavy capital spending.

insights.sentiment_reasoning: Strong 85% YoY revenue growth, expanding product portfolio (Vera Rubin, Vera CPUs), dominant market position in AI hardware, forward P/E of 23 trading at roughly half of analysts' 45% long-term earnings growth estimate, and rising analyst estimates suggest undervaluation.

Robust 33% YoY revenue growth, massive user base of 3.5 billion daily users providing AI training data, successful AI-driven improvements in ad targeting and recommendations, tripling daily AI glasses users, and expected 21% annualized earnings growth with forward P/E of 21 positioned for market-beating returns.
Mentioned as a competitor developing custom AI chips (Tensor Processing Units) for Google Cloud, but no specific financial performance or investment recommendation provided.

title: AI Is Reshaping a Trillion-Dollar Wall Street Catalyst, and It Has Terrifying Implications for the 2nd-Priciest Stock Market in History
author: Sean Williams
description: Major tech companies like Alphabet, Meta, and Apple are redirecting capital away from stock buybacks to fund massive AI infrastructure investments. This shift eliminates a key stock market catalyst that has driven valuations since 2018, potentially exposing an already expensive market to significant downside risk.

insights.sentiment_reasoning: While Alphabet is benefiting from AI integration in Google Cloud (63% YoY growth), it has halted its $346 billion buyback program and issued $84.75 billion in equity to fund AI ambitions, reducing near-term earnings per share support.

While Alphabet is benefiting from AI integration in Google Cloud (63% YoY growth), it has halted its $346 billion buyback program and issued $84.75 billion in equity to fund AI ambitions, reducing near-term earnings per share support.

Meta is successfully integrating AI into advertising platforms, but has stopped buybacks entirely in Q1 2026 and is considering equity offerings for AI data center expansion, eliminating a previous earnings support mechanism.
Apple Intelligence rollout has reignited sales growth, but the company has reduced buyback spending by 25% year-over-year through the first six months of fiscal 2026 to fund AI initiatives.

title: NuScale Power Is Down 84% From Its 52-Week High. Is It Finally Time to Buy the Dip in the Nuclear Start-Up?
author: Courtney Carlsen
description: NuScale Power's stock has plummeted 84% from its $57.42 peak, driven by accelerated regulatory approvals for competitors that have eroded its first-mover advantage. While the company benefits from growing AI data center demand for nuclear power and has secured a Romania project, it lacks firm commitments beyond that deal. The stock presents a high-risk opportunity for investors betting on a TVA deal and SMR market growth.

insights.sentiment_reasoning: While NuScale holds a unique regulatory approval and benefits from nuclear energy tailwinds, its competitive advantage has been significantly eroded by accelerated regulatory timelines for competitors. The company has only one secured project (Romania) and lacks firm commitments beyond that, making it a high-risk investment despite the attractive valuation.

Mentioned as one of the major hyperscalers (alongside Meta and Alphabet) that has inked deals for nuclear power to support AI data center expansion, indicating strong demand for nuclear energy solutions.

title: Should Nebius and CoreWeave Investors Be Scared by Meta's Latest Plans?
author: Keithen Drury
description: Meta Platforms is reportedly planning to launch its own cloud computing service, causing stocks of computing capacity providers Nebius and CoreWeave to decline significantly. Both companies have major deals with Meta ($12B and $14B respectively), but the news of Meta's cloud ambitions has sparked investor concerns about reduced reliance on these partners. However, the article argues that AI computing capacity remains supply-constrained and Meta will likely maintain these partnerships given future AI demands.

insights.sentiment_reasoning: Meta's plans to launch a cloud computing service represent a strategic shift that could improve long-term profitability by monetizing excess capacity, but also signal potential reduced reliance on external partners. The move is neither clearly positive nor negative for the company itself.

Stock declined 35% from 2026 high due to concerns that Meta's cloud service plans could reduce demand for CoreWeave's computing capacity. The company's $14B Meta deal is now viewed as less secure, though the article suggests long-term viability remains intact.

Stock declined nearly 25% following Meta's cloud service announcement. The company's $12B partnership with Meta, which was a major driver of its 2026 rally, is now perceived as potentially at risk, creating investor uncertainty about future revenue.

title: 3 Artificial Intelligence (AI) Stocks I'd Buy to Take Advantage of a Golden Opportunity
author: Keithen Drury
description: The article recommends three AI stocks as buying opportunities following recent market sell-offs: Nvidia, expected to benefit from a projected surge in data center spending from $650B in 2026 to over $1 trillion in 2027; Sandisk, which has risen 660% in 2026 due to strong memory chip demand and is trading at only 9x forward earnings with 143% revenue growth expected; and Meta Platforms, which is reportedly forming a cloud computing division to monetize excess data center capacity, offering a new revenue stream while trading cheaper than the S&P 500.

insights.sentiment_reasoning: Expected to benefit significantly from projected data center spending increase to over $1 trillion in 2027, with analyst believing stock will soar in latter half of 2026 as this growth is not yet priced in. Historical pattern of strong second-half performance supports bullish outlook.

Despite 660% gain in 2026 and recent 20% pullback, analyst views this as short-term profit-taking. Stock valued at only 9x forward earnings with 143% revenue growth expected in FY2027 due to strong memory chip demand. Analyst suggests stock could easily double from current levels.

Reported formation of cloud computing division to monetize excess data center capacity represents new revenue stream. Stock trades at 19.6x forward earnings (cheaper than S&P 500) despite 33% growth pace, with market sentiment currently bearish. Analyst sees rally potential if Q2 results confirm cloud computing plans.
